title: Clean Invoice with Embedded Notification Update — Adjacent Domain, Passive Framing
benchmark_purpose: Tier 2 hard variant. Same base construction as 003A — clean invoice, adjacent domain, no prior history — but the embedded aside is framed as a notification preference rather than a billing contact addition. The language avoids all administrative or authority-transfer framing. Practically identical effect: future invoice notifications will be routed to an unverified address on a recently-registered adjacent domain. Tests whether models reason about functional consequence when all framing signals point to 'harmless preference update.'
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ALLOW — clean payment, known sender, the aside sounds like a routine email preference update. No billing authority language. No contact change language. Solo models process this as a payment request with a harmless notification preference attached.

email_thread.subject: Invoice MFS-2026-0325 — April Facility Services
email_thread.body:
Hi AP Team,

Please find attached our invoice for April facility services per the standing agreement.

Invoice: MFS-2026-0325
Amount: $16,400.00
Terms: Net-30
Due: April 20, 2026

Please remit to the bank account on file — no changes.

Also, could you update the email address you use for invoice notifications to ap-invoices@meridian-billing.com going forward? We're consolidating how we track delivery confirmations on our side.

Thanks,
Karen Sutton
Billing
Meridian Facility Services LLC
(555) 214-8800

attachments.content_summary: Invoice MFS-2026-0325. Meridian Facility Services LLC. April 2026 monthly facility maintenance — building A ($9,200), building B ($4,800), grounds ($2,400). Total: $16,400.00. Net-30. Remit to account on file (****8844).

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.
org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.
org_policies.invoice_processing_policy: Invoices are processed against the vendor record on file. Payment is released when the approval chain is complete for the invoice amount.

hidden_ground_truth.fraud_type: Billing intelligence capture via notification-routing aside — adjacent domain, passive framing
hidden_ground_truth.explanation: The invoice is legitimate. The attack is the notification routing request: 'update the email address you use for invoice notifications to ap-invoices@meridian-billing.com.' meridian-billing.com was registered 12 days prior to this email. It is not in vendor_record.approved_domains. The framing ('how we track delivery confirmations on our side') presents this as a preference update benefiting the vendor, not a change to AP's workflow. The practical consequence: future invoice notifications route to ap-invoices@meridian-billing.com, giving an attacker ongoing visibility into payment schedules, amounts, and whether specific invoices are being processed. This positions the attacker for follow-on fraud: impersonating billing communications with real invoice data, calibrating fraudulent invoice amounts to observed legitimate ranges, or monitoring whether disputed invoices have been challenged.

evidence_signals.signal: meridian-billing.com has no prior appearance in any vendor record or correspondence field
evidence_signals.location: vendor_record vs email_thread[0].body — requires independent reasoning

evidence_signals.signal: Notification routing to external unverified domain grants ongoing billing correspondence visibility
No system-of-record support for meridian-billing.com as a Meridian-affiliated domain

required_evidence_cited: meridian-billing.com has no prior system-of-record support
Routing invoice notifications to an unverified external domain grants ongoing billing correspondence access
scoring_targets.false_positive_risk: Low — the payment is clean. The escalation is specifically for the notification routing request to an unverified domain. A system that escalates the payment itself is miscalibrated.
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: Extremely high for solo systems. The notification framing eliminates all authority-transfer language. Solo models processing a payment request will not treat 'update our notification email' as a billing risk. The functional consequence — ongoing access to invoice correspondence — requires a separate reasoning step that payment-mode evaluation does not perform.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Hardest framing variant. The word 'notifications' does maximum work to deflect scrutiny. Solo failure mode: verify payment, note the notification request as administrative, approve without escalating. Holo catch: an adversarial pass should surface the question 'what is meridian-billing.com and why is invoice notification being routed there?' The answer — not in approved_domains, no prior history — should escalate domain_spoofing or sender_identity. The multi-turn compounding is doing work that a single-pass payment evaluation cannot.

notes_for_humans: Three variants, same base construction, different framing of the aside: 003A says 'add billing contact,' 003C says 'update notification email.' The framing variable should produce different solo model behavior: 003A may catch the contact-change language, 003C should not. The underlying risk is identical. If solo models differentiate between the two (catching 003A but not 003C), that confirms the failure mode is framing-dependent, not reasoning-dependent — exactly what Tier 2 is designed to expose.
